citation_sentences: The mechanical properties of the present steel in the as-quenched state as well as different cold rolling conditions are illustrated in Fig. 1. The dark dash lines in Fig. 1(a) and (b) connect the necking point of different samples. In the as-quenched state, the steel was characterized by a low yield strength (YS) and ultimate tensile strength (UTS) of \~570 MPa and \~ 830 MPa, respectively. But it exhibited a dramatic uniform elongation (UE) of exceeding $7 5 \% ,$ which meant the excellent work hardening capability. With the increase of cold rolling reduction, the YS of material gradually increased and eventually reached $\mathrm { t o } \sim 2 . 0$ GPa that is approximate 3.5 times of the as-quenched state. Simultaneously, the materials lost its work hardening capability, resulting to the decrease of the UE. Interestingly, the true stress of necking point was similar at low to medium rolling reduction (<50%), while it was sharply enhanced in the further rolling process.

paper_title: Evolution of microstructure, texture and mechanical properties of Fe–30Mn–11Al–1.2C low-density steel during cold rolling
citation_sentences: The optical micrographs of cold rolled microstructure are shown in Fig. 3. According to the morphology features, the microstructure evolution could be subdivided into two stages: (I) In the first stage (10% to 50% rolling reduction), the microstructure was the typical low energy dislocation structure (LEDS) of Taylor-lattice-type which was the result of the planar dislocation glide (Fig. 3(a-c)). The different distance between slip bands in different grains indicated that the deformation was inhomogeneous and more pronounced in favorable orientation (Fig. 3 (a)). The characteristic of this stage is the gradual refinement of slip bands space. (II) In the second stage (70% to 90% rolling reduction), compared with stage I, a plenty of shear bands were observed, and the grains were visibly elongated along the rolling direction (RD) (Fig. 3(ce)). The shear bands increased with cold rolling reduction and increasingly eroded the matrix to almost all of it. Therefore, the feature of this stage is the gradual development of the shear bands.

In present work, the dislocation planar slip (as in Cu–Mn alloy) dominates the deformation. In addition, deformation twins (as in Cu–Zn alloy and TWIP steel etc.) are also detected (Fig. 3-6). And the twining can also be indirectly reflected by the development of CuT after 50% rolling reduction (Fig. 9(b)) and the slight strengthening of γ-fiber texture (Fig. 9(c)). Specifically, the size of twins is nano-scale, forming the fine T-M lamella structure (Fig. 5(d) and Fig. 6(a)). The shifts of Cu and CuT (after symmetrical treatment) indicated by the red and blue arrows in Fig. 9(b) are consistent with the latent hardening process. Furthermore, the microshear bands generated by the local strain concentration are extensively observed, with accompanying by the rapid increase of the G component (Fig. 3(c-e) and Fig. 6(b)). In conclusion, above two mechanisms may be both contributed to the transition from “Copper-type texture” to “Brass-type texture”.

paper_title: Evolution of microstructure, texture and mechanical properties of Fe–30Mn–11Al–1.2C low-density steel during cold rolling
citation_sentences: Except the optical microscopy (OM), transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was also utilized to investigate the microstructure evolution in detail (Fig. 4-6). It is clear that the Taylor-lattice consists of the multiple slip bands (Fig. 4). The dislocations in the slip bands were approximately parallel, and the dislocation pile-ups were found at the intersections of the slip bands, as indicated by the green arrows in Fig. 4 (a). But the interaction of the non-coplanar slip bands was not strong to completely impede the dislocation glide. Consequently, the dislocation could across the intersection, making the slip bands across throughout the whole grain. It is no doubt that the Taylor-lattice structure is attributed to the planar dislocation glide [29], however, Sung-Dae Kim etc. [30] found that the dislocation cross slip might occur at the intersection, which could explain the weak interaction of the intersecting slip bands. Up to 30% rolled reduction, there was no obvious variation on structure except the gradual increase of the slip bands in Fig. 4(b). The average slip band spacing of sample of 30% and 50% reduction are measured as 231 nm and 64 nm, respectively.

When the true stress lower than \~1.2 GPa, the microstructure evolution is characterized by the refinement of the slip band spacing in the Taylor lattice (Fig. 4), which is similar to the tensile deformation microstructure evolution of Fe–30.4Mn–8Al–1.2C (wt%) [9,14]. The Taylor lattice structure is associated with the dislocation planar glide, which is widely considered as the results of “glide plane softening” effects [12,29].

citation_sentences: However, the microstructure changed significantly when the reduction reached 50% as shown in Fig. 5. The single slip band was hardly distinguishable. In addition, microbands were formed (indicated by the pairs of green dashes line in Fig. 5(a)) with the average width of 166 nm. The different contrast between microbands and matrix reflects that there is a misorientation among either side. Moreover, in some grains, the intermittent linear structures in parallel arrangement were detected, as shown in Fig. 5(b). According to the corresponding selected area diffraction pattern (SADP) (Fig. 5(c)) and the dark-field TEM micrograph (Fig. 5(d)), they turned out to be deformation twins. These twins were very fine with an average thickness of \~5.1 nm. Furthermore, it should be noted that different diffraction contrast was found in the single twin, which means the high dislocation density in twin [31]. When the reduction reached 70%, the size of twins increased to 19.2 nm (Fig. 6(a)) and many shear bands appeared in the microstructure (Fig. 6 (b)). The shear bands comprised of the ultrafine grains, confirming by the inserted diffraction ring pattern. Besides the increase of shear bands and twins, the dislocation tangles structure with the average size of 0.49 μm was developed as shown in Fig. 6(c).

When the stress reaches \~1.5 GPa, microstructure of microbands and its intersections are well established except the further refinement of the slip bands (Fig. 5(a)), which greatly resembles the tensile deformation structure of Fe–28Mn–9Al–0.8C (wt%) after failure [12]. The bound aries of the microbands are geometrically necessary dislocations (GNDs) that are formed to accommodate the misorientation between adjacent boundaries [12]. Additionally, the novel nano-sized deformation twins are detected, as shown in Fig. 5 (b-d), which is barely reported in the

In present work, the dislocation planar slip (as in Cu–Mn alloy) dominates the deformation. In addition, deformation twins (as in Cu–Zn alloy and TWIP steel etc.) are also detected (Fig. 3-6). And the twining can also be indirectly reflected by the development of CuT after 50% rolling reduction (Fig. 9(b)) and the slight strengthening of γ-fiber texture (Fig. 9(c)). Specifically, the size of twins is nano-scale, forming the fine T-M lamella structure (Fig. 5(d) and Fig. 6(a)). The shifts of Cu and CuT (after symmetrical treatment) indicated by the red and blue arrows in Fig. 9(b) are consistent with the latent hardening process. Furthermore, the microshear bands generated by the local strain concentration are extensively observed, with accompanying by the rapid increase of the G component (Fig. 3(c-e) and Fig. 6(b)). In conclusion, above two mechanisms may be both contributed to the transition from “Copper-type texture” to “Brass-type texture”.

paper_title: Evolution of microstructure, texture and mechanical properties of Fe–30Mn–11Al–1.2C low-density steel during cold rolling
citation_sentences: However, the microstructure changed significantly when the reduction reached 50% as shown in Fig. 5. The single slip band was hardly distinguishable. In addition, microbands were formed (indicated by the pairs of green dashes line in Fig. 5(a)) with the average width of 166 nm. The different contrast between microbands and matrix reflects that there is a misorientation among either side. Moreover, in some grains, the intermittent linear structures in parallel arrangement were detected, as shown in Fig. 5(b). According to the corresponding selected area diffraction pattern (SADP) (Fig. 5(c)) and the dark-field TEM micrograph (Fig. 5(d)), they turned out to be deformation twins. These twins were very fine with an average thickness of \~5.1 nm. Furthermore, it should be noted that different diffraction contrast was found in the single twin, which means the high dislocation density in twin [31]. When the reduction reached 70%, the size of twins increased to 19.2 nm (Fig. 6(a)) and many shear bands appeared in the microstructure (Fig. 6 (b)). The shear bands comprised of the ultrafine grains, confirming by the inserted diffraction ring pattern. Besides the increase of shear bands and twins, the dislocation tangles structure with the average size of 0.49 μm was developed as shown in Fig. 6(c).

When the stress increases to \~1.8 GPa, much microshear bands are generated due to the larger localized strain concentration (Fig. 6(b)). The shear bands can further refine the crystal structure because they consist of ultra-fine grains. Furthermore, at stress of \~2.1 GPa, dislocation cell structure is clearly observed (Fig. 6(c)), indicating that dislocation cross slip is operated.

The evolution of the texture must be associated with the evolution of the microstructure. Although the cross slip is found to occur at the intersections of slip bands in previous report [30] and is activated at higher rolling degrees in present work (Fig. 6(c)), the dislocation multiple planar glide dominates the rolling deformation, which is one of the striking feature of the present steel. This behavior is also observed in other alloys system, such as Cu–Ni [37], Ni–Cr [33], Cu–Mn [38] and LI ordered intermetallic alloys [39] etc. In Ni-based $\mathrm { L } 1 _ { 2 }$ ordered intermetallic alloys, the relation between the overall texture intensity and the difficulty of cross slip was investigated by a new concept of the width of the stacking-fault-like defect (SFLD) [39]. It is concluded that the more difficult the cross slip is, the weaker the texture is. The speculation that the planar slip may cause unusual crystal lattice rotation and thus weaken the intensity of texture is pay for this. Therefore, in present work, the weak texture is related to the dislocation planar glide. Additionally, substructure of inhomogeneous deformation, such as microbands, shear bands and twins, may also scatter the texture.

In present work, the dislocation planar slip (as in Cu–Mn alloy) dominates the deformation. In addition, deformation twins (as in Cu–Zn alloy and TWIP steel etc.) are also detected (Fig. 3-6). And the twining can also be indirectly reflected by the development of CuT after 50% rolling reduction (Fig. 9(b)) and the slight strengthening of γ-fiber texture (Fig. 9(c)). Specifically, the size of twins is nano-scale, forming the fine T-M lamella structure (Fig. 5(d) and Fig. 6(a)). The shifts of Cu and CuT (after symmetrical treatment) indicated by the red and blue arrows in Fig. 9(b) are consistent with the latent hardening process. Furthermore, the microshear bands generated by the local strain concentration are extensively observed, with accompanying by the rapid increase of the G component (Fig. 3(c-e) and Fig. 6(b)). In conclusion, above two mechanisms may be both contributed to the transition from “Copper-type texture” to “Brass-type texture”.

citation_sentences: In order to illuminate comprehensively the texture evolution, the intensity variations along α-, τ-, γ- and β-fiber are given in Fig. 9. In α-fiber (Fig. 9(a)), the {110}〈112〉 Brass orientation increased with cold rolling reduction and scattered to the {110}〈100〉 Goss position, which led to the gradually development of the whole fiber. Additionally, the maximum intensity position of the fiber shifted from $\boldsymbol { \Psi } ^ { 1 } = 4 5 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { t o } 2 0 ^ { \circ }$ at the higher rolling reductions (>50%), as indicated by the red arrow in Fig. 9(a). As shown in Fig. 9(b), the main orientation of τ-fiber was located in Cu and Goss positions. The intensity of the Cu orientation rapidly increased to maximum at 50% rolling reduction and then decreased. Meanwhile, with the decrease of Cu orientation, the Ф shifted to the higher values (as shown by the red arrow in Fig. 9(b)), and the Goss orientation also increased rapidly. Moreover, weak {552}<115 > CuT component was detected at 70% rolling reduction, and its Ф spread to lower values after 90% rolling reduction, representing by the blue arrow in Fig. 9(b). The γ-fiber with different rolling reductions remained weak (Fig. 9(c)). It was slightly strengthened at 90% reduction, which was relatively significant at {111}〈110〉 E position. The β-fiber started from B through S to Cu components is plotted in Fig. 9(d). It reveals that the Cu orientation increased first and then decreased, while the Goss orientation monotonously increased. The variation of S orientation was similar with Cu orientation. But the increase was significantly slower except the drastic increase at the 50% to 70% rolling states and the decrease delayed to 70% rolling reduction. The Ф and φ of maximum intensity position along β-fiber are displayed in Fig. 9(e) and Fig. 9(f), respectively. According to the maximum position of $\Phi _ { 2 } = 9 0 ^ { \circ } ,$ , it could be noticed that there was a continuously shifted from B towards Goss with the increase of cold rolling reduction.

In present work, the dislocation planar slip (as in Cu–Mn alloy) dominates the deformation. In addition, deformation twins (as in Cu–Zn alloy and TWIP steel etc.) are also detected (Fig. 3-6). And the twining can also be indirectly reflected by the development of CuT after 50% rolling reduction (Fig. 9(b)) and the slight strengthening of γ-fiber texture (Fig. 9(c)). Specifically, the size of twins is nano-scale, forming the fine T-M lamella structure (Fig. 5(d) and Fig. 6(a)). The shifts of Cu and CuT (after symmetrical treatment) indicated by the red and blue arrows in Fig. 9(b) are consistent with the latent hardening process. Furthermore, the microshear bands generated by the local strain concentration are extensively observed, with accompanying by the rapid increase of the G component (Fig. 3(c-e) and Fig. 6(b)). In conclusion, above two mechanisms may be both contributed to the transition from “Copper-type texture” to “Brass-type texture”.
